California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 20, 1989

John P. Mott-Smith
Outreach Coordinator
Office of the Secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-89-108

Dear Mr. Mott-Smith:

This will confirm our telephone conversation of
February 8, 1989, with respect to oral advice given regarding
a mass mailing. Specifically, the question centered upon a
possible exception to Government Code Section 89001 contained
in our Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed).

The mailing in question involved program materials to be
sent to high school principals, teachers of certain subjects
(i.e. art teachers), and to prior participating schools.
These materials were to be sent in conjunction with a joint
program of the Secretary of State and the State
Superintendent of Public Instruction. The telephone advice
given was that the mailing would come within the exception
set forth in Regulation 18901(f)(2) as a mailing "sent in the
normal course of business from one governmental entity or
officer to another governmental entity or officer."

Since then, we have reviewed Regulation 19801 in light
of the well-established rule of construction that exceptions
to a general rule are to be narrowly construed. (Estate of
Baneriees (1978) 21 Cal.3d 527, 540.) Therefore, should you
have occasion to send another mass mailing involving the
"governmental entity" exception, it would be advisable to
review the proposed mailing with us prior to distribution.

Finally, please remember that the exception noted above
is limited to mailings to the governmental entity or officer.
Materials provided to the schools for distribution to the
students would not qualify under this exception. Enclosed
please find a copy of a recent advice letter which discusses

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

John P. Mott-Smith
March 20, 1989
Page 2

the secondary distribution issue and which you may find
helpful.  (Alquist Advice Letter, No. I-89-109.)

     If you have any questions, you may contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

By:  Margaret W. Ellison
     Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:MWE:aa

Enclosures

Office of the Secretary of State
March Fong Eu

1230 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

ELECTIONS DIVISION
(916) 445-0820

For Hearing and Speech Impaired
Only:
(800) 833-8683

February 14, 1989

Margaret Ellison
Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Ellison:

This is to confirm our telephone conversation of February 8,
1989 during which we discussed laws regulating mass mailings.

As we discussed, the Secretary of State, in conjunction with
the State Superintendent of Public Instruction, conducts an annual
program in California schools to (1) encourage eligible 18 year
olds to register to vote and (2) to stimulate students of all
grade levels to think and talk about democracy. This program is
known as "You've Got the Power" and was initiated in 1988.

My question to you concerned whether the mass mailing
restrictions in Government Code section 89001 would prohibit the
use of Secretary of State Eu's or Superintendent of Public
Instruction Honig's name in mass mailings sent to high school
principals and teachers. These mailings would contain program
materials and information. Specifically, I asked whether or not
these mailings would be exempt from the restrictions of Section
89001 under the exemption provisions for mailings sent to other
governmental agencies or officials.

This is to confirm my understanding of your response to my
inquiry. You, in consultation with Mr. John McClean of your
office, have informed me that mailings from our office to school
principals and teachers are mailings to other governmental agen-
cies and as such are exempt from the restrictions of Section
89001.

I appreciate your quick response to my questions.

Sincerely,

DEBORAH SEILER
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform

JOHN P. MOTT-SMITH
Outreach Coordinator

JPM-S/gw

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 21, 1989

John P. Mott-Smith
Outreach Coordinator
Elections Division
Office of the Secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Letter No. 89-108

Dear Mr. Mott-Smith:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on February 16, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Margaret Ellison an attorney in the Legal
Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329.)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel
